title: SEGMENT 7 — Anti-PD-L1: hitting the ligand instead of the receptor

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Anti-PD-L1: hitting the ligand instead of the receptor
So far we have talked about three drugs that block T-cell receptors — ipilimumab blocking CTLA-4, pembrolizumab and nivolumab blocking PD-1.
But there is another way to interrupt the same signaling axis.
Instead of blocking the receptor on the T cell, you can block the ligand on the tumor.
You can hit the white flag instead of the eye that sees it.
That is the strategy of the anti-PD-L1 drugs — atezolizumab (brand name Tecentriq, from Genentech/Roche), durvalumab (Imfinzi, from AstraZeneca), and avelumab (Bavencio, from Pfizer/Merck KGaA).
All three are antibodies against PD-L1.
All three were approved between 2016 and 2017.
All three are workhorses, particularly in lung cancer, bladder cancer, and Merkel cell carcinoma.
Why bother with anti-PD-L1 if you already have anti-PD-1?
Three reasons, and they're worth understanding.
First, PD-1 has two ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2.
Anti-PD-1 antibodies block both interactions, because they sit on the receptor.
Anti-PD-L1 antibodies block only the PD-L1 axis.
They leave the PD-L2 axis intact.

Now, PD-L2 expression is more restricted than PD-L1 — it's mostly on antigen-presenting cells and some tissues like the lung — and there is a theoretical argument that leaving PD-L2 intact preserves some normal immune regulation.
Whether this matters clinically is still debated.
The toxicity profiles of anti-PD-L1 drugs are roughly similar to anti-PD-1 drugs, with maybe slightly less pneumonitis in some studies — possibly because PD-L2 signaling in the lung is preserved.
The data are not airtight.
Second, the antibodies hit different cell populations differently.
Anti-PD-1 sits on T cells.
Anti-PD-L1 sits on tumor cells, and also on tumor-infiltrating myeloid cells, which also express PD-L1 and which are major sources of T-cell suppression.
There's an argument that hitting the ligand provides a broader release of suppression in the tumor microenvironment.
Again, whether this translates clinically is unclear.
Third, and perhaps most importantly, patents and commercial competition.
When Merck and BMS got there first with anti-PD-1, the rest of the industry needed a way in.
Anti-PD-L1 was the available alternative target.
Roche, AstraZeneca, and Pfizer all built their immuno-oncology platforms around anti-PD-L1 drugs, and those drugs found niches.
The most striking anti-PD-L1 result, in my view, is the PACIFIC trial of durvalumab in unresectable stage III non-small-cell lung cancer.
These were patients with locally advanced lung cancer who had completed concurrent chemoradiation.
Historically, after chemoradiation, you'd watch and wait, and most patients would relapse and die within a few years.
PACIFIC added durvalumab as consolidation — given for a year after chemoradiation.
The five-year overall survival went from about thirty-three percent in the placebo arm to forty-three percent in the durvalumab arm.
A ten-percentage-point absolute improvement in five-year survival, in a setting where the standard of care had been stagnant for two decades.
That kind of result rewrites a treatment paradigm.

Atezolizumab, meanwhile, has been the workhorse anti-PD-L1 in bladder cancer and in triple-negative breast cancer, although its trials in some of those settings have had a rockier history — withdrawal of some accelerated approvals, debate about which subgroups benefit.
The history is messy.
But the principle is sound.
The takeaway: anti-PD-L1 drugs work, they hit the same biological axis from the other side, and clinically they are largely interchangeable with anti-PD-1 drugs in many settings.
The choice is often determined by which drug the company has and which combinations have been studied.
The mechanism is the same in spirit.
Take away the white flag, the T cells stop accepting the stand-down signal, the killing resumes.
